title: Needle felted bee
subtitle: A compact striped bee in yellow and black merino with gauze wings
excerpt: Needle felt a small bumblebee roughly 4 cm long from yellow and black merino, with a blunt furry thorax, a striped abdomen, and two wings cut from organza or stiff gauze. The bee works well as a brooch back, a plant stake decoration, or a gift tag ornament.
sourceNotes: Needle-felted insect miniatures are a well-established beginner genre in contemporary craft instruction. Method draws on standard ball-and-oval construction with surface-stripe technique.

attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees over a foam pad. Keep fingers clear of the path of the needle.
attrs.title: Needle safety

content.text: The bee is two sections: a roughly spherical furry thorax and a slightly larger oval striped abdomen, joined end-to-end. The wings are not felted; they are cut from stiff organza and glued or stitched on last. Stripes are added as
of black roving across a yellow core.

items.name: White or cream stiff organza or gauze (wings)
Strong thread or a dab of craft glue (for wings)

content.text: Roll 3 g of yellow roving into a sphere about 2 cm in diameter. Needle-felt to a firm but slightly fluffy ball: the thorax should be less dense than the abdomen so it reads as the fuzzy, furry section of the bee. Leave some loft in the surface rather than smoothing it completely flat.

Roll 4 g of yellow roving into an oval about 3 x 2 cm. Needle-felt to a firm, smooth finish with the size-38 needle, then refine the surface with the size-40. The abdomen should be clearly smoother and denser than the thorax.

Pull very thin strips of black roving and lay them across the abdomen at roughly even intervals to form two or three stripes. Needle-felt each stripe flat with the size-40 needle, working along the stripe in short stabs. The stripe should sit level with the yellow surface, not raised above it. Work around the full circumference of the stripe so it wraps the abdomen evenly.
Step 4: Join and add the head

Needle-felt the thorax to one end of the abdomen by stabbing through the join from multiple angles. Roll a small sphere of black roving (about 1 g) for the head and attach it to the other end of the thorax in the same way. The head should be noticeably smaller than the thorax.

Cut two wing shapes from organza: each roughly teardrop-shaped and 3 cm long. Pinch each wing at its base to create a slight pleat, then stitch through the pleat into the top of the thorax using strong thread, or secure with a small dot of fabric glue. Position the wings so they splay upward and outward from the thorax at about 45 degrees.

items.fix: Pull the black roving much finer before laying. Needle-felt by stabbing straight down along the edge of the stripe to define it cleanly.
items.cause: Roving strips laid too thick or needle moved sideways rather than straight in-and-out
items.problem: The black stripes have ragged, unclear edges

items.fix: Return the bee to the foam mat and stab through the join with the size-38 needle from all four sides, pulling fibres from each section into the other.
items.cause: Join not felted deeply enough from multiple angles
items.problem: The thorax and abdomen keep separating

items.fix: Use stiffer gauze or starched organza. Always pleat the wing base before attaching so the wing angles upward rather than lying flat.
items.cause: Organza too soft, or wings attached flat rather than with a pleat
items.problem: The wings droop rather than splay
